summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the children live with the Mother – Where one child is yet to meet the Father and one child is spending professionally supervised time with the Father – Where both parents make allegations of family violence against the other – Where the recommendations of the Panel Family Consultant are contingent upon the Court’s findings in relation to family violence on a particular occasion when the Mother was pregnant with the youngest child – Where there are significant factual disputes between the parents as to what occurred on that occasion – Where the fact of inconsistencies in the Mother’s recollection does not mean that she has lied – Where certain findings are made about the use of family violence – Where the Mother seeks that the children spend no time and have no communication with the Father – Where the Father poses a low risk to the children and the Mother – Where the Mother’s emotional and psychological safety will be protected by certain conditions – Where the Father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) propose a sensible, child-focused and gradual progression for the Father’s time with the children – Where orders made broadly in the terms sought by the Father and the ICL – Where orders made for the children’s surnames to be changed – Where orders made restraining the Mother from leaving the children in the unsupervised care of her older son or the maternal grandmother

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

BACKGROUND

These are parenting proceedings concerning X, aged six, and Y, aged two. Their parents are the applicant mother, Ms Trevaskis, and the respondent father, Mr Goodwin. Both parents have children from previous relationships, and none of those children are the subject of these proceedings. Y was born after his parents separated. Y and X live with Ms Trevaskis, Ms Trevaskis’s mother and their four maternal siblings in the maternal grandmother’s home. Three of those siblings were the subject of proceedings in this Court, which were joined with these proceedings but were resolved by consent, resulting in a shared care arrangement between Ms Trevaskis and their father, Mr L. X has been spending professionally supervised time with Mr Goodwin since about September 2023. Y has not met Mr Goodwin.
X’s and Y’s interests were represented in these proceedings by an Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”). I was told that the ICL had met and spoken with X on 25 November 2025 and that Y was present on that occasion.
Ms Trevaskis and Mr Goodwin had an “on and off” relationship, which commenced in about October 2018, and during which they were engaged to be married twice. They separated first in about April 2021 when X was two and Y not yet conceived. After that separation, X initially did not spend time with Mr Goodwin other than briefly while Mr Goodwin was removing his belongings from the parents’ former home and on one occasion in mid-2021 for Mr Goodwin’s birthday. In August 2021, she started spending time with Mr Goodwin for short periods, supervised by Ms Trevaskis in the company of her new partner, and then by M Contact Centre. Mr Goodwin commenced parenting proceedings on 9 February 2022. Following a mediation, Ms Trevaskis and Mr Goodwin reached final orders by consent, and these were made on 18 July 2022 (“the first final orders”), despite the parents having reconciled in about June 2022. Those orders provided for Ms Trevaskis to have sole parental responsibility for X with respect to most matters, and for X to spend unsupervised time with Mr Goodwin in a gradually increasing regime, with her first overnight time taking place from Christmas Eve to Christmas Day 2022 (when X would be three years old) and then moving towards alternate weekend overnight time by the time X had turned four. The first final orders were never implemented, because the parents were again living together. Shortly afterwards, Y was conceived.
Ms Trevaskis and Mr Goodwin separated on a final basis in early January 2023, when Ms Trevaskis was pregnant with Y, following the events of the evening of 1 January and the early morning of 2 January. What occurred then is hotly in dispute between the parents, and, because of the seriousness of what is alleged by each of them, and the significance of what occurred to the decision which I need to make, much of the final hearing focused on those events. I deal with them below.
After those events, reciprocal provisional Apprehended Domestic Violence Orders (“ADVO”) were made on the application of police for the protection of Ms Trevaskis and Mr Goodwin from each other. Ms Trevaskis was also charged with common assault and assault occasioning actual bodily harm. In May 2024 at Suburb N Local Court, one charge was dismissed and, having pled guilty to the other charge, Ms Trevaskis received a conditional release order, with no conviction recorded. A final ADVO was made for the protection of Mr Goodwin, and that has now expired. The provisional ADVO against Mr Goodwin for the protection of Ms Trevaskis was withdrawn by police prior to the hearing listed at Suburb N Local Court in March 2024.
Mr Goodwin has a new partner, Ms G. They have lived together since August 2023. Ms G has two children, aged 16 and 10, from a previous relationship. Those children live with Ms G and Mr Goodwin, and spend time with their father. Mr Goodwin’s three children from a previous relationship spend time with him each alternate weekend, with additional time in the school holidays as agreed between Mr Goodwin and their mother.
Mr Goodwin filed a contravention application on 9 May 2023 alleging that Ms Trevaskis had breached the first final orders. Ms Trevaskis commenced these proceedings on 8 June 2023. The parents agreed for the purposes of s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) that the first final orders needed to be reconsidered, and they were suspended. Pursuant to interim orders made by consent on 19 July 2023, since about September 2023 Mr Goodwin has spent time with X for two hours a fortnight professionally supervised. Prior to the making of those orders, Mr Goodwin had not spent time with X since early January 2023. During her supervised contact visits with Mr Goodwin, X has on occasion also spent time with her paternal siblings. She has also met Ms G on two occasions. A notation to the interim orders provided that: “The Mother will consider facilitating [Y’s] introduction to the Father during his supervised time with [X] after [Y] attains the age of 6 months and for such period of time as the Mother deems appropriate in consultation with contact centre staff”. Mr Goodwin has not yet met Y.

ORDERS SOUGHT

By her Amended Application for Final Orders filed 9 October 2025, Ms Trevaskis sought that the children live with her, that she has sole decision-making authority with respect to the children, and that X and Y spend no time and have no communication with Mr Goodwin. She also (and inconsistently) seeks an order that the children’s time and communication with Mr Goodwin occur “as determined by the Court”. As recorded in the Family Report, Ms Trevaskis had indicated to the Panel Family Consultant her position that any such time between the children and their father occur only on a supervised basis, and that it be “identity only” contact. Her oral evidence was that she would prefer no time, but accepted that some time may be ordered, given X has already been spending supervised time with Mr Goodwin. Her position at the close of the hearing was that there should be no time.
By his Amended Response to Final Orders filed 1 October 2025, Mr Goodwin agreed that Ms Trevaskis should have sole parental responsibility for X and Y with an obligation to consult him about major decisions, take his views into account and inform him of the decision made within 14 days of making such decision. He sought a gradual introduction of unsupervised time with the children, progressing to every other weekend and each Wednesday from 4.30 pm to 6.30 pm, as well as some school holiday time. At the close of the final hearing, he adopted the Minute of Orders Sought by the ICL.
At the commencement of the final hearing, the ICL did not have a position in relation to orders sought because of the need to test the evidence about the events of 1-2 January 2023. Prior to final submissions, the ICL provided a Minute of Orders Sought. That provided for Ms Trevaskis to have sole parental responsibility with an obligation to inform Mr Goodwin and consider his views, and for the children to spend gradually increasing time with Mr Goodwin, commencing with supervised time for the purpose of introducing Y, and progressing to two nights a fortnight and school holiday time.
By the conclusion of the final hearing, both parents and the ICL agreed that the children’s surnames should be changed (X’s from X Goodwin and Y’s from Y Trevaskis) so that they have the same surname (Goodwin-Trevaskis). Both parents and the ICL agreed, at my suggestion, that, if I was to order that communication can occur between Ms Trevaskis and Mr Goodwin, that should occur using the parenting app or other electronic means nominated by Ms Trevaskis. Ms Trevaskis did not oppose an order restraining her from leaving the children in the unsupervised care of the maternal grandmother or unsupervised in the presence of their brother, K.
For the reasons set out below, I have made orders generally in the terms sought by the ICL and Mr Goodwin.

EVIDENCE

Ms Trevaskis relied upon the following documents:
(1) Her affidavit filed 20 October 2025;

(2) Her affidavit in reply filed 21 November 2025.

Mr Goodwin relied upon the following documents:
(1) His affidavit filed 10 November 2025;

(2) An affidavit of his sister, Ms O, filed 10 November 2025;

(3) An affidavit of his partner, Ms G, filed 10 November 2025.

I received the affidavits of Ms Trevaskis and Mr Goodwin into evidence without their annexures. I received the affidavits of the parties’ witnesses into evidence, including the annexure to Ms O’s affidavit.
On 5 April 2024, an order was made pursuant to s 102NA of the Act. Whilst he was self-represented for some time leading up to the final hearing, Mr Goodwin ultimately obtained representation pursuant to the Commonwealth Family Violence and Cross Examination of Parties Scheme. Accordingly, Ms Trevaskis was able to be cross examined by counsel for Mr Goodwin. Ms Trevaskis was in receipt of legal aid and was represented by counsel at the final hearing, who cross examined Mr Goodwin. Both parents were also cross examined by a very experienced counsel for the ICL. Counsel for the ICL had more than the usual work to do because counsel for Mr Goodwin did not properly test the evidence, frequently gave Ms Trevaskis the opportunity to give what was effectively further evidence in chief in response to open questions, and limited his closing submissions to about eight minutes in a complex parenting matter which had been heard over five days. Ms G and Ms O were cross examined.
I received into evidence the Family Report of Panel Family Consultant, Ms P, dated 13 December 2023, and the updated Family Report of Ms P, dated 1 October 2025. Ms P is a social worker. In addition to the material she read in the process of preparing her reports, the Panel Family Consultant had been provided with, and read, the parents’ trial material. The Panel Family Consultant was cross examined by counsel for Ms Trevaskis, counsel for Mr Goodwin and counsel for the ICL.
The recommendations of the Panel Family Consultant in the Updated Family Report, insofar as they concern the children’s time with Mr Goodwin, revolve around findings made by the Court in relation to what occurred on 1-2 January 2023. In the event the Court accepts Ms Trevaskis’s evidence about what occurred (including that Mr Goodwin intended to kill her or the baby), the Panel Family Consultant recommended that X and Y have recognition time with their father, implemented by way of long-term supervision orders. If, on the other hand, the Court gives “no, or minimal, weight to Ms Trevaskis’s position of family violence” over 1-2 January 2023, the Panel Family Consultant recommends that Mr Goodwin’s time with the children reach an alternate weekend model, but that such time be implemented by way of an “incremental plan” which is “slow, staged, and in line with both children’s developmental needs”. If the children do spend such graduated time with Mr Goodwin, it was recommended that “in conjunction with” the incremental increases in time, Ms Trevaskis engage with a family therapist who is trained in “trauma and complex family dynamics”. The Panel Family Consultant also recommended that the Court consider an Undertaking (presumably by Ms Trevaskis) that the children have no unsupervised contact with K, and a continuation of the Undertaking that X and Y have no unsupervised contact with their maternal grandmother. It was also recommended that Ms Trevaskis “self-refer” to Q Family Services.
One exhibit was tendered on behalf of Mr Goodwin. Numerous documents were tendered on behalf of Ms Trevaskis and the ICL respectively.
Each parent and the ICL filed a Case Outline. Prior to closing submissions, I received a document described as a “Dot Point Summary of Oral Submissions for the Applicant Mother” and a document entitled “Factual findings sought by ICL in relation to 2 January 2023 incident”.
I have read and considered all the material outlined above.

FAMILY VIOLENCE

On behalf of Ms Trevaskis, a finding was sought in closing submissions that Mr Goodwin used family violence towards her, including physical violence, and controlling and coercive conduct, culminating in the events of 1-2 January 2023 when she said that Mr Goodwin “deliberately or with wilful disregard for the safety of the mother and the parties’ unborn child ... committed an act of non-fatal strangulation” or, in the alternative, sought to harm Ms Trevaskis and place Y (who was in-utero) at risk of dying or sustaining serious injury.
Mr Goodwin made allegations that Ms Trevaskis used family violence against him- including physical violence on 1-2 January 2023. No relevant finding was sought in closing submissions.
This is a matter, where, as in Friseal & Friseal [2025] FedCFamC1A 102; (2025) FLC 94-257 per Christie J at [25]: “the resolution of disputed allegations was central to the conclusion about whether the resulting orders would promote the safety (including the safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect or other harm) of” the children and the mother. I therefore need to make findings in relation to the allegations of family violence where that is possible. I note that counsel for Mr Goodwin did not make any coherent submission about what findings about family violence were sought by him.
Findings of family violence, and particularly the findings sought on behalf of Ms Trevaskis in this matter, are in relation to very serious matters, and I must be comfortably satisfied about the findings I make on the balance of probabilities having regard to that seriousness – this being the standard of proof encapsulated in s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) and enunciated by Dixon J in Briginshaw v Briginshaw [1938] HCA 34; (1938) 60 CLR 336 at 362. This does not mean, however, that I can only make such findings if there is evidence which is corroborative of the evidence given by a parent to the effect that family violence has been used against them (Amador & Amador [2009] FamCAFC 196; (2009) 43 Fam LR 268).
I begin with the evidence about what occurred prior to the significant events of 1-2 January 2023. It is axiomatic that what might be called “incidents” of family violence should not be considered in isolation and without relevant context. In part this is because it is important to determine whether there has been a course of controlling and coercive conduct. Such conduct heightens future risk.

Prior to 1 January 2023

Mr Goodwin’s conduct towards Ms Trevaskis and the children

Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that, after about March 2019, Mr Goodwin stopped taking medication for depression and then his moods became unpredictable and he became quick to anger. She gave evidence that Mr Goodwin had been impatient and at times shown aggression towards the older children, shouted at them and frequently kicked their toys in anger. She alleged that he slapped his daughter, F, across the face. She said that Mr Goodwin was neglectful of her needs – including by failing to respond when she was unwell. She said that Mr Goodwin told her that he was sexually aroused when she was crying and had sex with her when she was crying.
It is agreed between the parents that there were significant difficulties in communication between them. Mr Goodwin admitted that he would withdraw and not respond to Ms Trevaskis. It was put in submissions on behalf of Ms Trevaskis that this was family violence, in the sense that it formed part of his controlling behaviour.
After the parent’s first separation in April 2021, Mr Goodwin commenced parenting proceedings. In those proceedings, Ms Trevaskis filed a Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk in which she alleged some verbal abuse towards her, the older children and X and some physically aggressive behaviour towards the older children. She also alleged that on 20 February 2020, when she was trying to go outside, Mr Goodwin grabbed and shoved her and took her keys. She recorded that, as a result, she sustained multiple bruises to her wrists and a large bruise on her chest. She also alleged Mr Goodwin threw her across the bed in January 2021 and grabbed her, shoved her and pushed past her in February 2021. She repeated those allegations in these proceedings.
Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that she had a photograph of a bruise on her chest, which she said was a bruise caused by Mr Goodwin’s physical assault of her in February 2020. The photograph was not tendered, but Mr Goodwin conceded that she had a bruise on her chest at the relevant time and said that it must have been caused in some other way. I accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that Mr Goodwin was physically violent towards her in February 2020, resulting in bruising to her chest. I do not believe that she deliberately concocted evidence, as would be required if I was to disbelieve her evidence about the photograph. Ms Trevaskis also gave evidence that Mr Goodwin pushed her when they were both in bed in January 2021. I cannot make findings about that and other instances of alleged pushing and shoving, as that evidence was neither sufficiently detailed nor tested.
After the parents’ first separation, Mr Goodwin contacted the fathers of Ms Trevaskis’s other children. It was Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that her relationships with both of those fathers were abusive. One of them does not spend time with his son, R. I accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that Mr Goodwin’s conduct in contacting these men – which in turn caused R’s father to contact his school – caused her fear and distress. There was no legitimate purpose in Mr Goodwin contacting the other fathers, and he admitted that he should not have done so. I find that Mr Goodwin’s conduct in this regard was the use of family violence against Ms Trevaskis.
Otherwise, I am not satisfied that Mr Goodwin has engaged in coercive or controlling behaviour towards Ms Trevaskis. In particular, I reject the submission that Mr Goodwin withdrawing from Ms Trevaskis and refusing to communicate with her is part of a pattern of controlling behaviour constituting family violence, as opposed to simply poor communication and relationship difficulties.
Mr Goodwin conceded that he sent some aggressive text messages and emails to Ms Trevaskis after the first separation. There was no other evidence about those text messages and so I cannot make findings about whether the sending of them constituted family violence.
I believe Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that, at times, Mr Goodwin became impatient and shouted at the children. That was in a context that, at times, the household included eight children. I am not satisfied on the evidence that he physically assaulted his daughter F. I am not satisfied that any of his conduct towards the children constituted family violence.
In relation to Ms Trevaskis’s evidence about what might have been sexual assaults on her by Mr Goodwin (by way of having sex with her when she was crying), the allegations were disputed by Mr Goodwin, the evidence was not properly tested, and no relevant findings were sought. This is all concerning. If a parent has sexually assaulted another parent, that is significant both in itself and to findings about whether controlling and coercive behaviour has been used. It is therefore highly relevant to issues of risk and otherwise to what outcome is in a child’s best interests. On the evidence before me I am unable to make any findings about whether Mr Goodwin used family violence connected with sexual behaviour – including sexual assault – against Ms Trevaskis.
Having regard to all of the findings set out above, I am not satisfied that Mr Goodwin engaged in a course of coercive and controlling behaviour towards Ms Trevaskis.

Ms Trevaskis’s conduct towards Mr Goodwin

Mr Goodwin gave evidence to the effect that Ms Trevaskis was financially controlling of him, and isolated him from friends and family. He also gave evidence that she punched him on three occasions.
Ms Trevaskis admitted that she had, in March 2021 and prior to the first separation, punched Mr Goodwin on the nose and also that she had slapped him on one occasion in February 2021. That was the use of family violence by her.
Mr Goodwin gave evidence that Ms Trevaskis punched him on the head knocking him out for 30 minutes in around March 2021. Under cross examination, he gave the following evidence:
Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: You didn’t pass out for 30 minutes, did you?

[Mr Goodwin]: I passed out, yes

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: For 30 minutes?

[Mr Goodwin]: Well, I don’t – it felt like 30 minutes, because I don’t know exactly what time I was punched. But I do know that I woke up after some time.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: So where you say: “I recall that approximately 30 minutes later I woke up with a bloody nose” – that’s not correct. Because you just said you don’t know how long it was?

[Mr Goodwin]: Well, approximately 30 minutes is probably incorrect to say in that manner. Because I don’t know exactly how long I was knocked out for.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: So why did you say 30 minutes?

[Mr Goodwin]: So – it felt like a long time.

Her Honour: Well, if you’re unconscious how could it feel like a long time? How did you know it was a long time?

[Mr Goodwin]: I don’t know, your Honour.

Her Honour: So did you just add “approximately 30 minutes” as – what – why did you add that?

[Mr Goodwin]: It’s just because it was – I felt like it was an estimate timeframe of how long it would have been considering [Ms Trevaskis] wasn’t in the room and [Ms Trevaskis] was downstairs cleaning. It’s just the timeframe that I felt that it could have been for.

Her Honour: So just a guess?

[Mr Goodwin]: Yes.

Her Honour: And probably an exaggerated guess?

[Mr Goodwin]: Maybe not exaggerated. But it was just – it was a guess.

Her Honour: Well, if you passed out for 30 minutes that’s a really serious medical event, isn’t it?

[Mr Goodwin]: Yes. Yes.

Her Honour: Did you have any consequences after it?

[Mr Goodwin]: No, just the bloody nose.

Her Honour: All right.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: You didn’t go to the doctor?

[Mr Goodwin]: No.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: You didn’t go to the hospital?

[Mr Goodwin]: No.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: Did you think you should get that checked out?

[Mr Goodwin]: I didn’t think of that, no.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: Because it didn’t happen, did it?

[Mr Goodwin]: It happened.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: My client punched you. You sat on the bed together, you cried, she apologised, and that was it?

[Mr Goodwin]: No.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: You never sought any medical attention for any of the punches, did you?

[Mr Goodwin]: Correct.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: Even the one in January 2023?

[Mr Goodwin]: Correct.
I do not believe Mr Goodwin’s evidence about this matter, and find that he made this evidence up in the first set of proceedings between the parents, and repeated it in these proceedings. I accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that she punched Mr Goodwin on the nose during a dispute about K in about March 2021 and that Mr Goodwin did not pass out.
I do not accept that Ms Trevaskis engaged in coercive or controlling behaviours towards Mr Goodwin. I reject his evidence to the effect that Ms Trevaskis had, during the first period of the relationship, isolated him from friends and family. That evidence was considerably undermined in cross examination, when Mr Goodwin conceded that he had maintained the relevant relationships.

The events of 1-2 January 2023

Ms Trevaskis’s evidence

Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that on 1 January 2023, she and Mr Goodwin attended a barbecue at her parents’ home. They argued before they left and after they returned. Mr Goodwin accused her of ignoring him at the barbecue and said to her “[f]uck your family. Your brother is a cunt and your Dad can get fucked. He didn’t even look me in the eye or hang up the phone to shake my hand. I will never respect your family and I will never go there and they are never welcome in my house again”. Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that she then told Mr Goodwin they needed to separate and that he needed to leave. He initially refused to do so, saying that he could not afford to move out but, having been asked again to leave, started packing some personal items.
Ms Trevaskis gave the following evidence in her affidavit about what then occurred:
Later that night I was in our bedroom crying and I could hear [Mr Goodwin] still drinking and eating and became concerned that at some point, he would eventually come in and try to have sex with me. I went into the doorway and again said “I can’t, I just can’t. I need you to go. Please leave. I don’t care where you go, your mum’s, your sister’s, just pack your things and go”. [Mr Goodwin] rolled his eyes and squinted them at me. He clenched his fists and slowly got up off the couch. I stepped out of the doorway so that he could walk through but he bumped into me with his shoulder as he entered the room. He picked up a bag and threw my denim jacket towards me. I said “please calm down”. He again rolled his eyes. I said “I just can’t believe we’ve ended up here again”. [Mr Goodwin] mumbled under his breath “this shit again”. [Mr Goodwin] turned away from me, towards his bedside table and was breathing deeply. I lightly slapped him on the back of his right shoulder to get his attention, and in the hope he’d turn back and talk to me about fixing things. [Mr Goodwin] turned to face me and his eyes were wide and his face looked angry. I thought he was going to hit me. His fists were clenched and he started to swing his arms around my head. I thought he was going to punch me. He looked to me to be in a daze.

[Mr Goodwin] had previously told me that when he ‘gets in a mood’ that I should hit or punch him to ‘snap him out of it’. As [Mr Goodwin] looked to be in a daze, I slapped [Mr Goodwin] lightly on his face to snap him out of it. [Mr Goodwin] then grabbed me around the neck with one hand and grabbed my hair with his other hand, he pushed me forcefully backwards, towards a cupboard, causing me to fall over. [Mr Goodwin] tore chunks of my hair out. I noticed [Mr Goodwin] shaking clumps of my hair out of his hands. I ran my hands through my hair, and more of it came out. I said, to him, “Oh my gosh, you’ve ripped my fucking hair out”. He didn’t acknowledge anything I was saying. [Mr Goodwin] then walked directly into me and picked up a small pile of clothes from the bed. I grabbed onto the clothes he was holding and threw them onto the ground.

[Mr Goodwin] then grabbed my hands and squeezed them really hard, whilst also pushing my wrists back towards my body. I felt immediate pain and pressure on my wrists. He then turned me and threw me onto the bed. I was on my back and [Mr Goodwin] climbed on top of me, his body weight pushing against my abdomen. I was [pregnant] with [Y] at the time. [Mr Goodwin] was also much bigger than me. He then pushed his hand hard into my chest where the base of my neck is and applied pressure. I panicked as I thought [Mr Goodwin] was trying to hurt me and kill the baby ([Y]).

I was kicking my legs in the air and tried to push my knees up to get [Mr Goodwin] off me. Due to [Mr Goodwin’s] body weight on top of me, it was difficult to move. I do not recall how [Mr Goodwin] got off me, but the next thing I remember was him standing at the end of the bed, holding my leg with both hands and hitting himself in the head and face with my heel with force. I felt pain to my throat, my head, abdomen, chest and arms. I tried to get up but couldn’t as [Mr Goodwin] was holding my leg. He kept hitting himself in the face with my foot, particularly near his [eye]. At one point I thought he was going to knock himself out. I said words to the effect of “What are you doing, you psycho?”

I continued to try and get up. At one point, [Mr Goodwin] looked dazed and slightly lost his balance. He let go of my leg with one hand. I bent my knee towards myself and [Mr Goodwin] followed, leaning forward and trying to grab onto my leg again. I reached up as he got close and scratched his face as hard as I could. He then completely let go of my leg and put his hands up to his face, and I got up. I noticed then that I was drenched, and thinking my waters had broken, I said “Oh my God I am wet, if anything has happened to the baby, this is on you”. [Mr Goodwin] smirked at me, and said words to the effect of, “I’m going to say this whole thing was self defence”. He then took his blanket and pillow off the bed and left the room. I yelled out “I can’t feel the baby”. [Mr Goodwin] didn’t respond. I was laying down on the bed and tried to calm my breathing. I drank some water. My heart was racing. I tried to encourage movement of the baby but I could not feel anything. I remember screaming out, “I can’t feel the baby, you’ve killed him!”. [Mr Goodwin] didn’t say anything. I was confused, scared and crying. I Googled ways to make the baby move and how long to wait before contacting the hospital. The baby then got what felt to me like hiccups. I messaged [Mr Goodwin’s] sister what had happened. [Mr Goodwin] came into the room and asked if there was movement. I said I thought the baby was hiccupping. [Mr Goodwin] put his hand on my abdomen and I immediately felt scared. [Mr Goodwin] touched my abdomen for a few seconds and then walked out of the room to the couch just outside the room. Shortly after, [Mr Goodwin] fell asleep and when I heard him snoring I took photos in the dark of the chunks of hair [Mr Goodwin] had ripped out and some of the marks on my leg.

That night, I dozed off briefly and woke up to see I had lost control of my bladder. I realised that I had most likely lost control of my bladder during the incident as well. This happened again during the night. The incident was the first time I had lost control of my bladder in my adult life, as far as I can remember. I also felt pain in a lot of areas on my body including my wrists, legs, abdomen, chest and throat.
X was present in the house during these events, as was Ms Trevaskis’s son R. Y was present “in utero”. The parents assume that both children were both asleep during the events – although Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that X woke and came into her bed afterwards.
In response to an open question from counsel for Mr Goodwin, Ms Trevaskis gave a very compelling and believable oral account of what occurred on the night of 1 January and the early hours of 2 January 2023. It was evident that recalling what occurred was traumatic for her.
Ms Trevaskis readily admitted to police that she had first slapped Mr Goodwin on the back and then on his face after he had turned around. She also admitted both to police and in her affidavit to slapping him and punching him in 2021. During an interview with police in January 2023, she was resistant to making a statement and said “[n]o, it’s my own fault because I did slap him first” and “I feel extremely guilty because it was me who slapped him first and I just worry”. Even when she telephoned NSW Health in January 2023, she is recorded as having said “she had a ‘fight’ with her partner [...] and it was ‘my fault because I slapped him first’”. Very soon after what occurred, she sent a message to Mr Goodwin’s sister in which she admitted that “I slapped him because he was being arrogant”.
Ms Trevaskis pleaded guilty to common assault in relation to first slap – being the slap on the back of Mr Goodwin’s shoulder – although she sought to traverse that plea. She was found not guilty of assault occasioning actual bodily harm in relation to the slap to the face. In relation to the common assault, no conviction was recorded, Ms Trevaskis was put on a conditional release order and a 12-month ADVO was made for the protection of Mr Goodwin.
Ms Trevaskis’s evidence was not properly tested by counsel for Mr Goodwin, but aspects of it were tested by counsel for the ICL using documentary and other evidence available. I refer to inconsistencies in her evidence below.

Mr Goodwin’s evidence

Mr Goodwin gave the following evidence in his trial affidavit about what occurred after the family returned from the barbecue:

In the early hours of 2 January 2023, [Ms Trevaskis] and I had an argument resulting in [Ms Trevaskis] shouting at me and demanding that I pack my bags and leave the house. Prior to [Ms Trevaskis] demanding I leave the house in anger, I had already been contemplating leaving myself, but I hadn’t been able to think of anywhere to go as it was very late at night.

I can only think that this happened when I landed on the bed and my hand was used to soften the landing. [Ms Trevaskis] has very long hair and it was not tied up on this night. I always recall [Ms Trevaskis] easily losing her hair when caught or when [X] had pulled at her hair. Furthermore, [Ms Trevaskis] even admits in her affidavit to losing her hair from [Y] pulling at it in paragraph 124.

I then tried to leave the room, and it was at this point that [Ms Trevaskis] stood in front of the closed bedroom door and would not allow me to leave. Due to the attack that had just concluded I just stood at the door in [sic] hope she would move. After a few minutes I recall sitting on the bed and then [Ms Trevaskis] joining me.
