Later in the affidavit, he gave evidence that he had “simply acted in self-defence.”
Although in his trial affidavit he did not admit to doing so, under cross examination Mr Goodwin admitted that he had said to Ms Trevaskis prior to the events in the bedroom, “[f]uck your family. Your brother is a cunt. Your dad can get fucked”. His evidence in his trial affidavit – prepared after Ms Trevaskis’s trial affidavit had been filed – was deliberately evasive: “[a]fter returning home [Ms Trevaskis] and I argued where I was outspoken about how I was made to feel at her parent’s [sic] house ... ”.
In cross examination by counsel for the ICL, Mr Goodwin admitted that it was possible that at some stage while he was on top of Ms Trevaskis, he put his hand on her chest.

Key factual disputes

The key factual disputes between the parents about what occurred on 1-2 January 2023 therefore are:
(1) When Mr Goodwin turned towards Ms Trevaskis after she slapped him on the back, did he make physical gestures consistent with him being angry?

(2) Did Ms Trevaskis punch Mr Goodwin on the left side of his face (as well as slapping him on the face)?

(3) Did Mr Goodwin then grab Ms Trevaskis near or around the neck or did he immediately grab her by the wrist or wrists?

(4) When and how was Ms Trevaskis’s hair pulled out – by Mr Goodwin grabbing her around the neck, or by Mr Goodwin falling on her hair as he landed on the bed? Was Ms Trevaskis’s hair first in Mr Goodwin’s hand before they were on the bed or under his body on the bed after they had fallen?

(5) If Mr Goodwin grabbed Ms Trevaskis near or around the neck, did he attempt to choke her?

(6) Did Mr Goodwin grab one or both of Ms Trevaskis’s wrists, or did he grab her hands and push them back towards her arms?

(7) After Mr Goodwin grabbed Ms Trevaskis’s wrist/s or hands, did he turn her and deliberately push her onto the bed by way of an attack, or did Ms Trevaskis pull him onto the bed on top of her by way of an attack on him, or did they simply lose their balance and both fall on to the bed?

(8) Was Ms Trevaskis attacking Mr Goodwin with her feet while he was laying on top of her on the bed, or was she flailing to try and get him off her?

(9) Did Mr Goodwin accidentally or deliberately put his hand on Ms Trevaskis’s throat or chest while she was on the bed? Was he trying to choke her?

(10) Did Mr Goodwin use Ms Trevaskis’s foot deliberately to hit himself in the eye?

(11) After what occurred, did Ms Trevaskis block the bedroom doorway preventing Mr Goodwin from leaving?

(12) Has Ms Trevaskis used what occurred in a manipulative way to keep the children from Mr Goodwin?

(13) Did Mr Goodwin intend to kill or harm Y or Ms Trevaskis? Did Ms Trevaskis believe, and does she continue to believe, that he so intended?

Documentary and other evidence

In evidence were other accounts of what Ms Trevaskis is recorded to have said took place on that night and into the early hours of the morning. Those accounts were in contained in the following:
video recordings of two police interviews with her in January 2023, including a police statement;
a record of NSW Health “Maternity Screening Referral” in January 2023;
progress notes recorded by a social worker with NSW Health in respect of an appointment in January 2023;
a transcript of evidence which Ms Trevaskis gave in Local Court proceedings in February 2024, in which she was the defendant;
some paragraphs of affidavits filed by Ms Trevaskis on 8 June 2023 and 27 March 2024.
Photographs were tendered which showed:
(1) Light bruising on Ms Trevaskis’s left and right arms and both legs (taken after 2 January 2023);

(2) Strands of hair on a wooden floor;

(3) The layout of the bedroom where the events occurred;

(4) Light bruising on Ms Trevaskis’s sternum (taken on 6 January 2023);

(5) Bruising and a long red mark on and above Mr Goodwin’s eye; red marks on his cheek; blood on his ear; red marks on his neck and arm (taken on the morning of 2 January 2023).

A record of a General Practitioner (“GP”) provides that in January 2023, Ms Trevaskis had a “tender sternum”: “says where he put his hand and body wt”. The GP goes on to record that she had “bruise mid sternum – tender same site”.
In evidence were other accounts of what Mr Goodwin has said occurred that night. Those accounts were contained in:
A transcript of an ERISP interview conducted by police at Suburb N police station in January 2023;
A further interview conducted by police later in the evening in January 2023;
A transcript of Mr Goodwin’s evidence in the Local Court proceedings in February 2024, in which Ms Trevaskis was the defendant;
Soon after the relevant events of 1-2 January 2023, Ms Trevaskis sent Mr Goodwin’s sister, Ms O, some text messages, which Ms Trevaskis subsequently unsent. She then sent the following further messages from 2.42 am:
[Ms Trevaskis]: I know I shouldn’t have but I slapped him because he was being arrogant and he grabbed my hair ripped chunks of it out and then grabbed me by the neck. I was trying to get free and hitting/scratching his arms and then he threw me onto the bed and pinned me down and grabbed my leg and his other arm was holding my body down so I couldn’t get up. He was using my foot to hit himself in the head over and over and I was trying to push him away with my other leg but couldn’t so I scratched at his face and he let go. He was bleeding from my nails but he put a lot of pressure on my abdomen. I don’t know if it was pressure on my bladder or what but I was completely wet. It hurt a lot and I didn’t feel the baby for ages and have had a sharp pain at the front. The baby got hiccups not long ago but he was saying he will tell everyone what happened was self defence and I was the one attacking him the whole time. I did slap him first but he’s a psychopath. He lied in his last affidavit that I knocked him unconscious and left him there for 30 minutes, which absolutely did not happen. He thinks I recorded an argument to use in court so now he wants to try to make me look like I’m violent. I’ve never hit anyone before him. He pushes and pushes and pushes and enjoys watching me break. I hate him.

[Ms Trevaskis]: I’m sure he is feeling the scratches but my neck really hurts. I have bruises on my legs, mostly the one he used to hit himself and bruises and scratches all over my forearms, wrists and hands.

[Ms Trevaskis]: Plus if he hurt the baby, I’ll never forgive him.
Ms O gave evidence in her affidavit that in the deleted text messages, Ms Trevaskis had admitted that “she had become very angry with my brother and that she was kicking and hitting him while he was on the ground gathering his belongings”. Ms Trevaskis gave evidence about those messages as follows:
In the very early hours of 2 January 2023, after the incident between me and [Mr Goodwin], I messaged his sister [Ms O] a number of very short messages in panic and said words to the effect “he’s killed the baby”, “he’s a fucking psycho”, “I’ll never forgive him for this” and “I can’t feel the baby”. I unsent the messages shortly after I felt [Y] moving. I unsent the messages because I realised that it may be distressing for [Ms O] to wake up and see these messages.
Under cross examination by counsel for Ms Trevaskis, Ms O appeared to give evidence that she had never seen the content of the “unsent” messages, and appeared to clarify her evidence to the effect that the text messages to which she was referring in her affidavit were the messages from Ms Trevaskis sent at 2.42 am, which were not unsent, and which are in evidence before me. None of those messages involve an admission by Ms Trevaskis that she “had become very angry” with Mr Goodwin, nor that she was “kicking and hitting him while he was on the ground gathering his belongings”. No one has made that allegation in these proceedings. Under cross examination by counsel for the ICL, Ms O gave different evidence – namely, that she had seen the “unsent” messages and that those were the messages to which she was referring when giving evidence about Ms Trevaskis’s admissions. Surprisingly, she gave evidence that she had not looked for the “unsent” messages between when she first saw them on the morning of 2 January 2023, and when she was preparing her affidavit in November 2025 and that she did not realise they had been “unsent” until November 2025. That was so notwithstanding that she knew the events of 1-2 January 2023 were in dispute between Mr Goodwin and Ms Trevaskis. Her evidence under cross examination by counsel for the ICL was:
Counsel for the ICL: If you knew that you had written evidence from [Ms Trevaskis] admitting to being angry with him and kicking and hitting him, why did you not provide that evidence to your brother?

[Ms O]: I didn’t know – I didn’t realise it was relevant at that time. I – to me, the Family Court had nothing to do with the abuse. It was- he was trying to go for custody to see his children. The trial about the- the abuse and everything- I didn’t realise it was going to be all brought up then and there. To me, in my opinion at that time, I felt it was two different incidences.
Subsequently (and inconsistently) she said that she didn’t want to be “this involved” and “just wanted peace”. She then changed her evidence again:
Counsel for the ICL: Didn’t you think that these messages that you say you saw on 2 January would have assisted in proving that your- or at least, in proving that your brother was not necessarily the one- the only one who was aggressive on that occasion?

[Ms O]: Look, of course it would have helped him. Absolutely, it would definitely have helped. Did I- I wasn’t even asked for it, even at that time either. Yes. I- I can’t answer that. I don’t know why I didn’t give it. I- to be honest with you, I can’t even remember the whole situation when it all first started with [Mr Goodwin] and the court proceedings.
Ms O’s evidence was confusing and unsatisfactory. If the “unsent” messages contained the content that Ms O alleged, then it is hard to believe that she would not have at least looked for them prior to drafting her affidavit to provide to her brother for the purpose of assisting him in these proceedings. Ms O admitted that she did not have a clear memory of whether she had told Mr Goodwin what the messages said. I prefer and accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence about the content of the messages which were unsent and the reason for her having unsent them. The messages support that Ms Trevaskis experienced what occurred on 1-2 January 2023 as an attack on her and unborn Y by Mr Goodwin.

Inconsistencies between the various accounts

The core aspects of Ms Trevaskis’s accounts were consistent. There were some inconsistencies – none of which were put to Ms Trevaskis by counsel for Mr Goodwin, but some of which were put to her by counsel for the ICL:
(1) In her trial affidavit, Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that, after she had slapped Mr Goodwin on the back of his shoulder, he turned around and “his eyes were wide”. To police in January 2023, she said that “[h]e had his eyes closed”. Under cross examination, she gave evidence that Mr Goodwin’s eyes were “really squinty”. In her first affidavit, she gave evidence that “[h]is eyes looked crazy”.

(2) In her evidence under cross examination, Ms Trevaskis said that after she had slapped him on the face, Mr Goodwin “started squeezing around my neck”. In her trial affidavit, she gave evidence that after she lightly slapped Mr Goodwin’s face, “[Mr Goodwin] then grabbed me around the neck with one hand”. When it was put to her by counsel for the ICL that she did not say in her affidavit that Mr Goodwin squeezed or applied any pressure to her neck, Ms Trevaskis at first said “I don’t know how much he did”, but then confirmed that her evidence was that he squeezed her neck. She said she could not remember whether she had difficulty breathing.

(3) There were inconsistencies between Ms Trevaskis’s evidence and what she was recorded as having told a social worker in January 2023. I do not attach any significance to those inconsistencies. I accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that the social worker was not writing anything down when they were talking. The notes themselves make clear that they were prepared retrospectively. I accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that the account which she gave to the social worker was not accurately recorded. Nonetheless, key elements of the account given by Ms Trevaskis on other occasions were included.

(4) Whilst it appears from a transcript of the criminal proceedings at Suburb N Local Court in May 2024 that, at least at some stage, it was part of Ms Trevaskis’s case that Mr Goodwin had given her permission to slap him, Ms Trevaskis is not recorded in the interviews with police and hospital staff in the immediate aftermath of 1-2 January 2023 as having told anyone that Mr Goodwin told her to slap or punch him. She gave evidence under cross examination that she had told the police what Mr Goodwin had asked her to do, but that is not included in any of the recordings or transcripts. In fact, when she was asked by police why she slapped Mr Goodwin the first time, she said “... I just got extremely frustrated and I was just hurt, I don’t know, I’m not even sure”. She also said “and for some stupid reason, I slapped him across the face”. She was specifically asked by police whether Mr Goodwin gave her permission to hit him and she said that he did not. She sent a message shortly after what occurred to Mr Goodwin’s sister which did not mention that Mr Goodwin had told her to hit him: “I know I shouldn’t have but I slapped him because he was being arrogant”. Ms Trevaskis’s first affidavit did not include evidence that Mr Goodwin had told her to hit him, and rather included evidence that she behaved in that way because she “feared greatly for my safety”. She did add evidence about what Mr Goodwin told her to do “when he gets in a mood” in her second affidavit filed 27 March 2024, though her evidence was that he had “often” told her to do so (rather than describing Mr Goodwin as having “previously” told her, as she ultimately deposed in her trial affidavit). Ms Trevaskis’s explanation under cross examination by counsel for the ICL was as follows:

Counsel for the ICL: [Ms Trevaskis], did you add that piece of evidence about [Mr Goodwin] telling you that when he gets in the mood you should hit or punch him to snap him out of it, did you add that to your affidavit in March 2024 to improve your case in these proceedings? To make it look like, or to attribute some responsibility to [Mr Goodwin] for why you slapped him on 2 January?

[Ms Trevaskis]: Not – no.

Counsel for the ICL: You sure about that?

[Ms Trevaskis]: Not to – not in – not the way that that’s described. I put it in there because I felt that the fact he had said it was relevant. I had been told previously by police that it was irrelevant. But along the way I did mention it to everyone, and everyone had a different opinion about it.

Counsel for the ICL: When you say you mentioned it to everyone, there’s no record of you mentioning it at the hospital or when you spoke to a social worker at the hospital [in January]. Would you agree with that?

[Ms Trevaskis]: There’s no record of it. Yes.

Counsel for the ICL: And there’s no record of you mentioning it to the police, although you say that you did. Correct?

[Ms Trevaskis]: Correct.
(5) In her trial affidavit, Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that Mr Goodwin “climbed on top of” her. Under cross examination, she said that he was not sitting on her To the social worker at the hospital, she is recorded as having said he was sitting on her.

(6) Until she made her second affidavit in March 2024 (and although not in identical terms to those used in her trial affidavit), Ms Trevaskis was not recorded as having made any reference to the detail that, when Mr Goodwin was hitting himself with her foot, he was doing so “particularly near his [eye]”. That detail was omitted from her earlier accounts and from her first affidavit. It was Mr Goodwin’s eye which was most visibly injured.

(7) In her first affidavit in these proceedings, Ms Trevaskis had given evidence that Mr Goodwin had not wanted Y and that she thought he was trying to harm Y deliberately. In her trial affidavit, she left out her previous evidence that: “[t]his was not a planned pregnancy, and when I told [Mr Goodwin] that I was pregnant I recall him saying, ‘Well I know that you don’t want abortions, so it is what it is’ ... I thought that he wanted to get rid of the child”. Nonetheless, her evidence at trial was clear that she believes (and believed at the time) that Mr Goodwin deliberately tried to kill Y. She is not recorded as having told police or the social worker who interviewed her in January 2024 that she believed Mr Goodwin had deliberately tried to hurt or kill Y. Initially, Ms Trevaskis admitted that she did not share this belief with police, but then said that she was not sure whether she had. She was of the view that she had told the social worker. That is unlikely, as the social worker would almost certainly have recalled and recorded such information if Ms Trevaskis had shared that belief. In the text messages she sent to Mr Goodwin’s sister just after what occurred, Ms Trevaskis did not suggest to Ms O that she believed Mr Goodwin deliberately tried to hurt or kill Y; the relevant message was “[p]lus if he hurt the baby, I’ll never forgive him.”

(8) Under cross examination by counsel for the ICL, Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that, after Mr Goodwin had grabbed her hands, he turned her first into a chest of drawers, and then turned her again and threw her on the bed. She conceded that she had not given that evidence in her affidavit, in which the evidence was to the effect that Mr Goodwin grabbed her hands, pushing back on her wrists, and threw her onto the bed. Her evidence under cross examination was, however, consistent with the evidence she had given in the Local Court in February 2024. I do not regard this as a significant inconsistency, given that it involved only the omission of some detail about what occurred between the grabbing of her hands and pushing back of her wrists and the pushing onto the bed.
Counsel for the ICL asked Ms Trevaskis about some inconsistencies in her accounts and asked “[w]ould you agree with me that your memory of the incident was clearer then than it is now?”. She answered:
[Ms Trevaskis]: I honestly don’t know how to answer that, because there have been parts that I feel like I remembered clearly as time went on, more – more parts that I had forgotten initially, and I also feel like there are parts that I don’t remember as clearly now that I did then.

Counsel for the ICL: How have you remembered parts more clearly as time has gone on?

[Ms Trevaskis]: I remembered extra things that were said throughout the night that I hadn’t recalled, or I hadn’t mentioned. I don’t know why at the time.

Counsel for the ICL: Were there particular things that caused you to remember things as time went on?

[Ms Trevaskis]: I stayed within the home for a little while longer, and as I was packing, some things were triggered after that; and there were also other times when I would just be doing random things or having an interview with someone about the incident, and it would come to mind. Some of the things I just felt, because it’s a lot of just what was said and there was no actual physical thing happening, I’ve just not added to it to add confusion, but there have – there have been things that I’ve remembered from being triggered.
The Panel Family Consultant gave oral evidence that inconsistencies, both in relation to “variance in the facts” and in other aspects, can be explained by the experience of trauma:
[Ms P]: ... it’s variance in the facts, it’s variance in – it can change over time. Interpretation of the experience, memory recall, emotions attached to the incident. There’s quite a lot of different variances sometimes in traumatic experiences, due to the impact it has on the brain and memory recall.
I have not assumed that, just because there were some inconsistencies in Ms Trevaskis’s accounts, she is deliberately not telling the truth. In any case, the core elements of Ms Trevaskis’s account were consistent.
Similarly, the core aspects of Mr Goodwin’s account on the different occasions contained in evidence before me were consistent. There were the following inconsistencies:
(1) Mr Goodwin’s evidence under cross examination was that he only grabbed one of Ms Trevaskis’s wrists, but photographs in evidence show bruising on both of her wrists.

(2) In his trial affidavit, Mr Goodwin gave evidence that “I grabbed [Ms Trevaskis’s] legs”. Under cross examination, he gave evidence that he grabbed only one leg – her right.

(3) In an affidavit of 23 June 2023, Mr Goodwin gave evidence that “I grabbed [Ms Trevaskis’s] legs to stop her hitting me, and got up from the bed and walked out of the room”. In his trial affidavit and in cross examination, he said that he had not been able to leave the room because Ms Trevaskis was blocking the door. He could not offer an explanation for this difference.

(4) In the immediate aftermath of what occurred Mr Goodwin did not allege that Ms Trevaskis deliberately pulled him on top of her by way of an attack – although he did say that he was pulled onto her.

Findings in relation to 1-2 January 2023

Notwithstanding the compelling oral evidence of Ms Trevaskis, I am unable to be satisfied to the requisite standard that some things occurred as she said they did. That does not mean that Ms Trevaskis has lied about what occurred. I accept that, she was pregnant and therefore vulnerable, she experienced what occurred as traumatic and, at various stages, has come to believe that Mr Goodwin was trying to harm her and to kill yet-to-be-born Y that night.
Ms Trevaskis has experienced considerable childhood trauma and adversity, and in adulthood has experienced trauma in other relationships, including physical violence from her mother and from her son. She has recalled past physical abuse and continuing verbal abuse from her father. When asked about Ms Trevaskis’s recollection of what occurred on 1-2 January 2023, the Panel Family Consultant gave the following evidence about on Ms Trevaskis’s memory:
[Ms P]: It [trauma] also affects a part of the brain which is called the hippocampus, and the hippocampus is the part of the brain that codes memories, makes sense of memories, retains memories, and what happens when you have had persistent trauma throughout your life, is that those memories can often become fragmented or confused, and often we might experience past triggers or traumas within current scenarios, because those memories are so fragmented due to the hippocampus being affected. So, overall, trauma, especially if it has been chronic, if it has been from childhood, throughout intimate relationships, and if there’s current stress and trauma, that’s persistent, and it’s enduring trauma, and it’s – it can have a significant impact on the brain and, therefore, memory, yes.
The Panel Family Consultant also agreed with the proposition that “[i]t can take some time ... for someone who has been through a traumatic incident, to process that incident, and they might regard it as more traumatic later than at the beginning”. It appears to me that this is what has occurred for Ms Trevaskis.
I find that the parents argued verbally when they returned home from a family barbecue on 1 January 2023. Mr Goodwin used foul language to describe Ms Trevaskis’s father and brother, but there is no evidence that she was coerced or controlled by his use of that language, nor that it caused her fear. It was therefore not family violence within the meaning of s 4AB of the Act.
I find that Ms Trevaskis slapped Mr Goodwin lightly on the back of his shoulder to get his attention while he was turned away from her and bent down to get items from a bedside table. He then stood up and turned around so he was facing her. I find that Ms Trevaskis then slapped Mr Goodwin on the lefthand side of his face. I am not satisfied that this was done as an act of self-defence nor that Mr Goodwin had given Ms Trevaskis permission to slap or punch him. It is inherently unlikely that Mr Goodwin gave Ms Trevaskis that permission, and, given the opportunity by police to say whether Mr Goodwin gave her permission to slap him, she said that he had not. Her text message to Mr Goodwin’s sister is inconsistent with that being the reason she slapped Mr Goodwin. Mr Goodwin is, on a number of occasions, recorded as having denied that he ever gave her such permission. She likely slapped Mr Goodwin on the face in frustration at him not engaging with her. That was the use of family violence by Ms Trevaskis.
I am not satisfied that Ms Trevaskis also punched Mr Goodwin, as he has alleged. She has readily admitted to slapping him, and to punching him on another occasion. She expressed remorse for both. With that history, I do not believe that she would lie about the punch on this occasion. I also take into account that Mr Goodwin has previously lied about Ms Trevaskis’s use of violence towards him. There were bruising and other marks to his eye, but they could have been caused by what occurred on the bed.
I am satisfied to the requisite standard that, after Ms Trevaskis slapped him, Mr Goodwin then grabbed her near or at her neck with one hand, and pushed her backwards. During that assault, some of Ms Trevaskis’s hair was pulled out by Mr Goodwin. I believe Ms Trevaskis’s account that she saw Mr Goodwin shaking her hair out of his hands (where it was subsequently photographed on the floor). That is also consistent with her message to Mr Goodwin’s sister. I am satisfied that Mr Goodwin did not squeeze Ms Trevaskis’s neck and I am satisfied that he was not attempting to choke or strangle her. Ms Trevaskis has not consistently made these allegations, and there was no bruising or other physical sign that this occurred. Mr Goodwin gave a credible denial of that conduct and intention. Ms Trevaskis did not attempt to leave the bedroom after she says this occurred, even though she admitted to counsel for the ICL that she could have done so. Rather, she continued to engage with Mr Goodwin – who was attempting to leave.
Mr Goodwin then attempted to take some clothes from the bed to pack and leave. Ms Trevaskis grabbed those clothes from him. Mr Goodwin then grabbed both of Ms Trevaskis’s wrists, applying enough force to lightly bruise them, and pushed her. Ms Trevaskis said that he pushed both of her hands backwards causing her considerable pain (something which she had previously experienced in childhood), but the evidence of the bruising in the photographs is more consistent with applying pressure to her wrists. Given the bruising, that likely caused her the pain which she recalls. The subsequent push by Mr Goodwin resulted in both parents ending up on the bed with Mr Goodwin on top of Ms Trevaskis. I do not believe that Ms Trevaskis pulled Mr Goodwin onto the bed as he alleged. They either fell or Mr Goodwin pushed Ms Trevaskis onto the bed. Mr Goodwin fell on top of Ms Trevaskis. I do not believe that Ms Trevaskis was attacking Mr Goodwin with her legs while laying under him on the bed. I find that Ms Trevaskis was trying to get Mr Goodwin off her by flailing her legs, that Mr Goodwin could easily have got off Ms Trevaskis by rolling off her and did not do so for several seconds. Neither he nor I understand why he did not. It may be, as the Panel Family Consultant opined, that he simply froze in the moment. I find that, while he was on top of her, he put his hand with weight on her chest. That resulted in light bruising to her sternum. I am satisfied that Mr Goodwin was not trying to strangle or choke Ms Trevaskis. I accept that, at times, Ms Trevaskis has genuinely believed that he was. I am satisfied that Mr Goodwin was not trying to kill or hurt Ms Trevaskis nor the unborn baby. He could have done more to avoid what occurred which might have endangered the baby. I do not accept that Mr Goodwin was acting in self defence. I accept that Ms Trevaskis has, at various times after what occurred, genuinely believed that he was trying to kill the baby and hurt her, and remains unsure about what Mr Goodwin’s intentions were.
I am not satisfied that Mr Goodwin deliberately used Ms Trevaskis’s legs to hit himself in the eye, but I accept that it may have looked like this to Ms Trevaskis, given he was holding one of her legs and that it likely contacted with Mr Goodwin while Ms Trevaskis was flailing. Mr Goodwin’s injuries likely occurred after the parents were both on the bed and while Ms Trevaskis was flailing to get free or scratching him to get free.
I am satisfied that Ms Trevaskis is genuinely fearful of Mr Goodwin and genuinely anxious about the children spending time with him – even if such time is supervised. Ms Trevaskis’s actions are a response to what she believes Mr Goodwin did to her on that occasion, and not because she is selfishly motivated to keep X and Y from having a relationship with Mr Goodwin, as he alleged. I do not accept that Mr Goodwin is fearful of Ms Trevaskis.
I accept the Panel Family Consultant’s evidence that Ms Trevaskis’s experience of anxiety in relation to Mr Goodwin is linked to prior traumatic experiences and “that might have led to her having a hypervigilant presentation”. The Panel Family Consultant explained:
[Ms P]: ... she’s experienced quite a lot of trauma. From her own family, from her previous partner, and up until now to her older children. So there’s no, you know, I have a lot of empathy for her, that she’s been through a lot. And so I can understand why she would have. A hyper-aroused nervous system is linked to trauma, because what happens is, trauma impacts the brain, or the flight or flight system. And when the fight or flight system is activated for a long time, the body feels it, and becomes anxious and fearful.
I accept that it is likely Ms Trevaskis’s presentation can be attributed to a whole range of life experiences, not just Mr Goodwin’s conduct towards her.

RELEVANT LAW (PARENTING ORDERS)

When making parenting orders, the best interests of X and Y are the paramount consideration. In working out what is in their best interests, I am required to have regard to the matters in s 60CC of the Act. As neither X nor Y are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander children, the matters that apply only to such children are not extracted below.
60CC How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests

General considerations

(2) ... the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.
(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.
I am also required to apply s 60CG of the Act, which provides:
60CG Court to consider risk of family violence

(1) In considering what order to make, the court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child’s best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order:
(a) is consistent with any family violence order; and

(b) does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the court may include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.

ANALYSIS AND FACTUAL FINDINGS S 60CC FACTORS

Safety

I have set out above my factual findings in relation to family violence used by the parents, including the events of the night of 1 January and the morning of 2 January 2023. I have found that both parents used family violence against the other. I am not satisfied that Mr Goodwin engaged in a course of coercive and controlling behaviour towards Ms Trevaskis. That is not to say, however, that the violence that occurred on 1-2 January 2023 was “mutual” and not relevant on that (or any other) basis. Ms Trevaskis was pregnant with Y, and physically smaller than Mr Goodwin. It was within Mr Goodwin’s capacity to simply walk away after Ms Trevaskis slapped him. Instead, he assaulted her in the ways which I have set out above. Ms Trevaskis experienced what occurred as traumatic, it could have endangered unborn Y, and its consequences have had significant repercussions for Ms Trevaskis, for the children and for the children’s relationships with Mr Goodwin.
I need to consider whether the children and Ms Trevaskis are safe from harm if the children spend unsupervised time with Mr Goodwin, taking account of the findings which I have made.
The Panel Family Consultant had carried out a careful risk assessment in this matter. It was her opinion that, unless I am satisfied that Mr Goodwin intended to kill Ms Trevaskis and/or the baby on 1-2 January 2023, there is a low risk of Mr Goodwin using family violence against Ms Trevaskis or the children.
The Panel Family Consultant gave oral evidence about how she had carried out the risk assessment, and identified that she had used a “family violence internal risk assessment that the panel family consultants follow”. She gave evidence that the assessment is “based on research, international research and national research. It’s aligned very much with the Safer Together Institute’s model of family violence risk assessment”. I accept the submissions made by counsel for the ICL about the Panel Family Consultant’s risk assessment evidence:
Counsel for the ICL: There were a number of factors that the consultant said that your Honour has to take into account in assessing whether or not there is an unacceptable risk in light of the historical facts: firstly, there is no evidence that the father has committed any other acts of violence; secondly, there is no evidence that there has been any family violence or allegations of family violence in any of the father’s other intimate relationships, other than the mother’s assertions.

Her Honour: Yes.

Counsel for the ICL: Importantly, there is no evidence of aggressive or violent behaviour by the father in other contexts. So work-related problems, no antisocial behaviour, no angry or aggressive communications with the school, no difficulties at the contact centre.

...

Counsel for the ICL: There is no evidence, apart from the mother’s assertions, that the father has been violent or aggressive towards any of the children in either of the households. There is no evidence of any involvement by DCJ in respect of the father or any of the – in respect of the father and any of the children in either of the households. There is no evidence of any current mental health issues affecting the father which might impact on his emotional regulation or impulse control. There is no evidence of any substance abuse issues that might impact currently on the father’s emotional regulation or impulse control. There is the unchallenged evidence that the father spends substantial time with the [Goodwin] children, and appears to have a flexible and informal arrangement with their mother.

Her Honour: Yes.

Counsel for the ICL: There is the undisputed fact that the supervised time with [X] over the last two years has been positive - - -

Her Honour: Yes.

Counsel for the ICL: - - - and that [X] has not reacted negatively either before or after that time. There are the positive observations of the family consultant, and her evidence that she could not identify any indicators of poor impulse control or emotional dysregulation, apart from the usual stress and anxiety of being in these proceedings. There was the father’s presentation in the witness box, where he was placed under very strenuous cross-examination, and he remained calm, distressed, but calm, and it is also reflected in the orders he seeks.
There are a number of aspects of the risk assessment that require further exploration.
The first is the fact that Mr Goodwin denies aspects of what I am satisfied occurred, and expresses no insight into the impact of what occurred on Ms Trevaskis, nor any sense of responsibility for what occurred. I asked the Panel Family Consultant about the impact of someone acknowledging their behaviour upon risk:
Her Honour: So presumably, though, an element that might alleviate risk is if someone acknowledges the way that they behaved on a particular occasion. That might cause one to be less concerned that if they – if I find they’ve falsely given an account about it?

[Ms P]: Yes, it would be concerning because part of a risk assessment is looking at all the things I’ve said, but it’s also about remorse, and accountability, and reflective capacity, and being open and transparent about what the incident really looked like, and the role that the parents played in that incident, and if there is a role, then taking accountability can then lead to therapeutic change and insight, and if there isn’t that accountability or that acceptance of the role that they had, then it causes concern that if there was a particular similar trigger in the future, for example, the root of the issue isn’t addressed.
The second is that the risk assessment relied in part upon the account given to the Panel Family Consultant by Mr Goodwin’s current partner, Ms G. Her evidence was thoroughly unsatisfactory. Mr Goodwin gave evidence that he had told her all about the allegations which had been made against him: “[w]hen [Ms G] and I started to become serious in our relationship, I openly told her about the situation involving [X] and [Y], the assault, the accusations against me and the ADVO”. Ms G also gave evidence in her affidavit that “[Mr Goodwin] also shared what had occurred between himself and [Ms Trevaskis] [sic] and informed me about the charges and AVO that were placed on him as a result”. However, under cross examination, she refused properly to answer questions about what she had been told by Mr Goodwin, and I find that she did so out of concern that something she may say would be inconsistent with Mr Goodwin’s evidence to the Court. The following extract from the transcript suffices to illustrate the point:
Her Honour: He hasn’t told you anything about what happened?

[Ms G]: Pardon?

Her Honour: He hasn’t told you anything about what happened?

[Ms G]: Not in great – no, he hasn’t.

Her Honour: Well, you said not in great detail. Has he told you in any detail? –

[Ms G]: Pardon?

Her Honour: Has he told you in any detail what ... ?

[Ms G]: -Not too much, no.

Her Honour: Well, what has he told you?

[Ms G]: - He just said that there was a court case going on between him and his ex, but he hasn’t gone into a lot of details about what had happened.

Her Honour: So you’re not being asked to provide a lot of details, just to say what he told you about that incident –?

[Ms G]: - He hasn’t –

Her Honour: - apart from that there was a court case?

[Ms G]: - said too much about it.

Her Honour: So when you say – I’m sorry, but when you say he hasn’t said too much, that suggests he has said something, and you’re being asked what he’s said?.

[Ms G]: - He has just said that there was a situation that has occurred, but he hasn’t gone into too much details. I haven’t pushed him for it. Everything that he has said has been based on what he’s looking [sic] in terms of the children, but that’s all we have spoken about.
It beggars belief that Mr Goodwin and Ms G have not had conversations of any substance about what occurred – particularly given that Ms G has two children who live with them when they are not with their father and that Ms G was aware that Mr Goodwin’s time with X has been supervised, and that he has not been spending any time with Y. Indeed, both Mr Goodwin’s and Ms G’s evidence in chief was to the effect that they had had detailed conversations about what had occurred. Despite the unsatisfactory nature of Ms G’s evidence, I have no basis to think that her account of her relationship with Mr Goodwin given to the Panel Family Consultant, and on the basis of which the Panel Family Consultant concluded that Ms G had not raised any concerns about Mr Goodwin, was not accurate. While the Panel Family Consultant agreed that it could “potentially” have been relevant to explore with Ms G what Mr Goodwin had told her about what had occurred, she explained that “the focus of the risk assessment, when I’m exploring it with the current intimate relationship, is their experience of the relationship, whether they’ve experienced any risk or violence”. The Panel Family Consultant was not particularly concerned about Ms G’s evidence in cross examination about her conversations with Mr Goodwin about what occurred on 1-2 January 2023 (as summarised for her by counsel for the ICL):
[Ms P]: I think it could be concerning, but there are multiple variables and factors to consider when assessing risk. So whether he told her about the ins and outs of the incident, whether that means the children are at future risk, is one thing, but there are many other considerations when assessing the actual risk to the children, and that’s what my focus of the report was.
Finally, there is the significant difficulty that the Panel Family Consultant’s recommendations (and risk assessment) was predicated on whether I preferred Ms Trevaskis’s account of what occurred on 1-2 January 2023, or Mr Goodwin’s account. As is clear from what I have said above, the fact-finding exercise has not been so straightforward. I am well and truly satisfied that Mr Goodwin did not intend to kill Ms Trevaskis or the baby, but I was concerned about how the findings I have made “fit” within the risk assessment. I had excellent assistance from counsel for the ICL in that regard.
The Panel Family Consultant was asked by counsel for the ICL about what her recommendation would be, in terms of Mr Goodwin spending supervised or unsupervised time with X and Y, if the Court did not find that Mr Goodwin had an intention to kill Ms Trevaskis or Y, but did find that Mr Goodwin “grabbed the mother by the wrist, pushed her, they ended upon the bed with his weight on her”:
[Ms P]: If the Court was to find that the father didn’t have intention to kill, but that there was a physical altercation which led to the mother being pinned or her wrist being held, I would rely on my risk assessment around the overall likelihood of future risk. And that is – there’s a number of considerations for risks that we look at. We look at behavioural patterns. We look at personality profiles. We look at what’s – the collateral material is suggesting – the police material, the Child Protection material, to get a real understanding of if the children are in the care of the father, what is the likelihood that they will experience significant substantial risk. And my assessment points to the fact that that’s low. There’s no previous patterns of violence or charges for the father. The father has two children in his full-time care. And I wasn’t made aware of any Child Protection concerns. The father’s partner in interview did not raise any concerns around violent behaviour. And he also has consistent time with his current children and there’s no Child Protection concerns around that. So if it is the case that there was a physical altercation, I would then rely on the overall likelihood of future risk, which in my assessment is low.
I explored further with the Panel Family Consultant her risk assessment as well as the question of risk, and counsel for the ICL then followed up, asking “[i]f her Honour was to find that the father did not intend to kill the mother or the baby, but that he did physically attack the mother, would your recommendation still be for unsupervised time ... or supervised, or something else?”. The Panel Family Consultant opined:
[Ms P]: I think you have to weigh it up in terms of the need of the children, and for their relationship with their father as well. If there was an incident of physical violence when the mother was pregnant, that’s a risk, and it needs to be mitigated and ameliorated through change programs, therapeutic programs, but is it enough to maintain a significantly cautious approach with [X] and [Y], that they don’t have a relationship with their father. If there’s no other evidence outside of that one incident to suggest the father has a personality profile that can get to that level of rage, or that level of aggression, I think it would need quite a lot of evidence to suggest this is the personality profile of this person, to sever a relationship between the children.
The Panel Family Consultant gave evidence about what she would be looking for in terms of Mr Goodwin’s personality profile and opined that “for this father, there wasn’t that evidence that I could see”. I agree with that assessment of the evidence, and it was also consistent with my observations of Mr Goodwin in the witness box. Importantly, I reiterate that am not satisfied that Mr Goodwin has engaged in a course of controlling or coercive behaviour towards Ms Trevaskis either during the relationship or subsequently.
Notwithstanding the matters explored above, I accept the risk assessment carried out by the Panel Family Consultant and the conclusion that, given the factual findings I have made in relation to the events of 1-2 January 2023, the overall likelihood of future risk in relation to family violence is low.
I am satisfied that the children are not at an unacceptable risk of harm of any kind if they spend unsupervised time with Mr Goodwin. I am satisfied that Ms Trevaskis is not at an unacceptable risk of family violence if the children spend unsupervised time with Mr Goodwin. I am satisfied that she experienced, and has continued to experience, what occurred on the night of 1-2 January 2023 as traumatic. I am satisfied that the risks to her emotional and psychological well-being posed by the children spending unsupervised time with Mr Goodwin and the requirement for her to communicate with Mr Goodwin can be mitigated in circumstances where she has the assistance of various professionals, and provided that she has the option to nominate a third party to effect changeover, where written communication is limited and is to occur by such means nominated by Ms Trevaskis, and where Y will be gradually introduced to Mr Goodwin in a supervised context, following which there will be a gradual progression of the children’s time with him. I am satisfied that the orders which I propose to make do not give rise to any relevant risk to Mr Goodwin.
It was conceded by Ms Trevaskis that her eldest son, K, has been physically violent towards her, Y and X, and used a knife to threaten Ms Trevaskis. She deposed in detail to the history of K’s difficulties, diagnoses (including of Oppositional Defiant Disorder and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder) and the steps which have been taken to assist him. K is now in a shared care arrangement with his father and Ms Trevaskis has had considerable assistance through the Department of Communities and Justice, as well as various organisations and professionals, in managing these issues. Ms Trevaskis and K’s father have a new treatment plan for K and I accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that she and Mr L are now working better together in relation to K. He is to be assessed for Autism Spectrum Disorder. The Panel Family Consultant opined that Ms Trevaskis “presented as a mother who is insightful and is aware of the dynamics that’s she’s managing ... she’s resourceful and doing the best she can”. The Panel Family Consultant also opined that Ms Trevaskis was “very aware of the risks in her own house”, was honest about that and “takes on feedback”. That is consistent with my assessment of the evidence, including the oral evidence which Ms Trevaskis gave and her presentation. Ms Trevaskis did not oppose an order restraining her from leaving the children unsupervised with K, and I am satisfied that there is no unacceptable risk to X and Y from K in those circumstances.
Ms Trevaskis and the children live with her mother, in her mother’s house. Ms Trevaskis conceded that her mother had assaulted her by grabbing her hair and slamming her head into a wall on 2 June 2024, and also that her mother had threatened self-harm in September 2023. The children were in the house during both incidents. I accept Ms Trevaskis’s evidence that her mother received professional assistance for approximately one year, continues to implement the strategies she learnt and is far more settled. Again, Ms Trevaskis did not oppose an order restraining her from leaving the children in the unsupervised care of her mother, and I am satisfied that this mitigates, to an acceptable level, the risk to the children from conduct of the maternal grandmother.
I accept the Panel Family Consultant’s evidence that an important aspect of risk minimisation in relation to K and the maternal grandmother is that another parent has “eyes on” the children, and that X and Y have some respite from Ms Trevaskis’s household.

X’s views

X told the Panel Family Consultant that she “’likes’” seeing her dad and said that she misses him when she doesn’t see him, explaining that this meant “’want them to be there, when they are not’”. When asked if she would like to see Mr Goodwin more, she said that she would. She also said that she would like to see her paternal siblings more. She said that she likes living with her mother, but reported being scared of her maternal sibling, K, who she said had been physically violent towards her. X is very young, and so her views are not relevant because she should get what she wants, but rather because they are an indication of her feeling loved, safe and comfortable with Mr Goodwin. That was consistent with the observations of the Panel Family Consultant.

Children’s needs and parental capacity

Y and X are very young, and Y has not yet met his father. As Mr Goodwin recognised, any introduction of unsupervised time needs to be gradual, and Y will benefit from being introduced to Mr Goodwin with the assistance of a supervision service and the professionals who work there.
Ms Trevaskis has faced considerable adversity in her life – including what I accept was abusive parenting and trauma during her childhood. She has undertaken considerable work with professionals to ensure that she is the best mum that she can be to her six children. She has undertaken parenting courses, including Tuning Into Kids and Emotion Coaching. She engages with a psychologist and a counsellor, with whom she is addressing her trauma and anxiety and completing the Circle of Security parenting program, and has also been engaging with what she describes as a “family law counsellor”. When it was put to Ms Trevaskis by counsel for the ICL that she was recorded in the second Family Report as having described herself as parenting Y and X with a soft and calm approach, she responded:
[Ms Trevaskis]: I didn’t say those words. They’re not words that I would use. But if I had to describe my parenting, it is calm and – I do – it’s gentle. I practise the emotion coaching that I’ve learned in the parenting courses.
The Panel Family Consultant observed Ms Trevaskis to be a warm, engaged and effective parent and concluded that she presented as “a warm, attuned, and kind mother who loves her children”. That was consistent with her oral evidence and presentation. The Panel Family Consultant identified concerns in relation to familial stressors and the potential impact of such stressors on X’s school attendance, and recommended Ms Trevaskis’s attendance at “an early intervention child protection programme, such as [Q Family Services]”. Ms Trevaskis has engaged with a number of programs, including S Family Services. I was not asked to make any order for her to attend any additional programs, and there is no need for me to do so when I am confident that Ms Trevaskis will continue to obtain appropriate support.
When asked about whether her anxiety has affected her ability to parent Y and X in any way, Ms Trevaskis again responded in a thoughtful manner:
[Ms Trevaskis]: In the beginning, I was affected by it. And I would shut off, and have to remove myself, and go and be alone a fair bit. But I have done a lot of work on myself with numerous different support workers. My counsellor, my victim services counsellor, I’ve had multiple people in for support that I’ve had – so to a far less degree now, if ever, have I had to do that ...

When my kids are around, I hope they see very little – if – I aim for none ...

I hope they see none. But I don’t obviously know what they perceive ...
To her credit, she gave evidence that she is now able to cope better in dealing with the father of her three oldest children, with whom she has agreed to a shared care arrangement.
Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that she continues to experience anxiety. She gave evidence that when X comes home from spending supervised time with her father, she often thinks about what occurred. When asked by counsel for the ICL about the symptoms of that anxiety, she gave the following evidence:
[Ms Trevaskis]: I get shortness of breath, sometimes I get a little bit clammy, I feel hot but also cold at the same time, sometimes my – I will actually shiver, but I don’t feel cold, if that makes any sense. And also just a little bit restless, and my heart – my heart beats a little bit more quickly ...

I’m getting better at managing my anxiety – sorry. I also get an eye twitch, it just happened then. It’s little triggers sometimes, even just getting into the car and doing the drive there reminds me of – obviously, what I’m doing, which is – and there’s a reason why the time is supervised, and then also when I’m sitting and waiting for [X], because I wait around the area for the visit to – sorry, for the two hours, and then driving also home, and then it just – it depends, there have been a couple of times where the centre has sent items home with us, which I had asked not to occur, however, there are some times where things do come home, and that can also trigger me as well.
Ms Trevaskis gave evidence that she is now less triggered by her relationship with Mr L than in the past: “But the last set of orders that were made with [Mr L], I’ve had no choice but to communicate with him a lot more than I used to as well, and I have found ways to make the communicating between myself and [Mr L] be a lot easier”. She also agreed that, in the past, her childhood trauma had been a trigger for anxiety, but gave evidence that “in recent times”, it was “not so much” of a trigger.
There was no expert evidence to the effect that Ms Trevaskis’s parental capacity would be undermined by orders for time, and no closing submission was made that this was a “Re Andrew” case (so-called after the matter of Re Andrew [1996] FamCA 43; (1996) FLC 92-692) such that I would order no time irrespective of risk because of the impact of an order for time on the mother and consequently on the children. Such a submission would not have been sustainable given the lack of any evidence from a psychologist providing treatment to Ms Trevaskis and also that the Panel Family Consultant opined she did not observe signs that X had “picked ... up” on her mother’s anxiety. Of Ms Trevaskis, the Panel Family Consultant said “I found her emotional in the interview, but I found her very appropriate in front of the children. I found her very good at regulating and calming herself, when she was around the children, and that was in both observations”. The Panel Family Consultant opined that, in the observations, it did not appear to her that Ms Trevaskis’s anxiety was having an impact on X and Y. I accept that evidence.
Mr Goodwin made various criticisms of Ms Trevaskis’s parenting in his affidavit, but did not seek any orders which were reflective of him having any real concern about those matters. He did not even seek a restraint in relation to K or the maternal grandmother until he joined with the ICL’s proposed final orders at the conclusion of the final hearing.
Mr Goodwin has three children from a previous relationship. They live in City T. He sees them every second weekend, with additional time in the school holidays. He drives to City T to collect them and brings them back to Sydney, often working on the coast on Fridays to make his drive shorter and enabling him to pick the children up directly from school at times. He sometimes drives them back to City T for sporting events on Saturday. He then drives them home to City T at the end of the weekend. These arrangements are by way of an informal agreement between him and these children’s mother and are testament to his commitment to his children. He gave child-focused evidence about his intention that initially X and Y’s time be on the weekend when he does not have care of their paternal siblings, so that he can focus on them.
Mr Goodwin admitted that, during the first part of his relationship with Ms Trevaskis, there were issues with how he communicated with her and dealt with his and Ms Trevaskis’s children. He undertook an intensive program of Neuro-Linguistic Programming, which he gave evidence improved both his ability to express his emotions and to communicate with the children. I accept that evidence. He has also undertaken counselling work and completed Parenting after Separation and Circle of Security courses.
In general, Mr Goodwin has taken a patient and child-focused approach to these proceedings. His proposals for time are sensible and child-focused and are consistent with him having good parenting capacity.
Mr Goodwin has patiently and consistently engaged in supervised time with X, for which he pays $100 a fortnight. A U Family Services report dated 1 August 2024 described familiar and affectionate interactions between Mr Goodwin and X. The Panel Family Consultant gave evidence that the supervision reports were consistent with her observations of X with Mr Goodwin: “I noted there was excitement from [X] when she saw her father, and there were signs of a positive relationship, a warm relationship, and appropriate interactions”. She provided further detail:
“[X] was quite excited to see her father, and they embraced when he walked into the room”.
“The father also used positive or warm names for [X]”.
“There was [sic] no signs that I observed of any particular hypervigilant, or anxious behaviours from [X]”.
“There was close proximity, there was [X] playing and then exploring the room on her own confidently, which is often a sign of a level of comfort, and there was playfulness and curiosity”.
“It was a nice observation”.
Ms Trevaskis conceded both to the Panel Family Consultant and to the Court that there is no negative reaction from X before or after supervised time and that she appears to enjoy her time with her father.
The parents do not have any co-parenting relationship, nor do they communicate directly at this stage. Mr Goodwin is highly suspicious and distrustful of Ms Trevaskis. He interprets everything that Ms Trevaskis does in a negative light. For example, he gave the following evidence:
I was also starting to question why [Ms Trevaskis] recommenced a relationship with me. Deep down, I felt [Ms Trevaskis] pursued a relationship with me to avoid letting go of [X] and seeing her flourish in a separate home outside hers.
He gave evidence that he believed that when Ms Trevaskis arranged with the mother of his other children for the siblings to meet, she did so to collude with her and gather evidence against him. He also gave the following evidence:
It is my view that following [Ms Trevaskis’s] assault on me on 2 January 2023, [Ms Trevaskis] has been refusing to facilitate spend time arrangements with me on the basis of her irritation with charges being laid against her or otherwise personal annoyance at me specifically and is not concerned for the best interests of [X] or [Y].
He confirmed under cross examination that that was still his belief. He gave the following rather extraordinary evidence:
I believe part of what made [Ms Trevaskis] snap into a rage on the night of the serious incident is that I had learnt tools to communicate properly. Tools to help get to the real root cause of problems. I feel [Ms Trevaskis] wasn’t coping with me being able to hold her accountable for the things she said and with me [sic] efforts to understand her reasoning.
Nonetheless, he also gave the following, more child-focused evidence:
Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: So you and [Ms Trevaskis] would need to be able to trust each other to communicate about [Y] and how he is coping with that, wouldn’t you?

[Mr Goodwin]: It would be through the contact centre.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: So you wouldn’t communicate with [Ms Trevaskis] about that?

[Mr Goodwin]: The initial stages, we wouldn’t need to communicate it. We – it would be through the contact centre, because then they can make sure that [Y] is progressing.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: But if [Ms Trevaskis] tells the contact centre, “[Y] is not coping well,” you wouldn’t trust her when she says that, would you?

[Mr Goodwin]: The – it – it would depend if I had the same belief, obviously, based on what I see. But outside of that, I – I wouldn’t be able to know whether or not [Y] is coping outside of the- the contact centre.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: So would you accept – if she gives the feedback that he’s struggling, you would accept that?

[Mr Goodwin]: Yes, and I will say that today. I will accept that, because that young boy, who knows how he’s going to react. He doesn’t know me.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: And sitting here today, you think that you will trust [Ms Trevaskis’s] judgment on that moving forward?

[Mr Goodwin]: I would have to trust her judgment.

Counsel for [Ms Trevaskis]: You would have to?

[Mr Goodwin]: I would have to, because it’s for [Y].
Ms Trevaskis is very anxious in relation to Mr Goodwin. This was obvious from her presentation in Court and consistent with the Panel Family Consultant’s assessment of her, which is that she “became quite emotional and anxious, when talking about the father, in particular, when talking about the incident”. As an example, she found that her anxiety was triggered by having a doll in the house that Mr Goodwin had gifted X.
The Panel Family Consultant gave evidence under cross examination, which I accept, that:
[Ms P]: ... [p]rotecting the children from any denigration, any negative comments, any negative commentary about either parent is crucial in terms of the trajectory of the relationship being positive. The children really need to be safeguarded from any level of negative commentary or denigration.
She opined that if the children are not safeguarded or protected from negative views: “... children often feel stuck in loyalty [binds], and if they’re stuck in a loyalty [bind], they often experience anxiety, and that can manifest in behavioural issues”.
I am satisfied that, now this litigation has concluded, Ms Trevaskis and Mr Goodwin will both be able to manage the relatively modest amount of communication which will be necessary to facilitate the graduated progression of time to two nights a fortnight and school holiday time sought by the ICL and Mr Goodwin. The Panel Family Consultant was relatively optimistic that the parents would work towards communicating in a positive manner. Having seen them in the witness box, I am satisfied that Ms Trevaskis will continue to, and Mr Goodwin will, protect the children from the damage to which the Panel Family Consultant refers.
The Panel Family Consultant opined that it would be of benefit to X and Y to have some respite from their mother’s household, and also to their benefit for Ms Trevaskis to receive extra parenting support so as to avoid “carer burnout”. In cross examination, her evidence was:
[Ms P]: So my assessment of the mother from the first family interview up until the second family interview, is that I think she’s a mother who is doing very well, who cares about her children, but there is [sic] quite a lot of stresses around her, and that’s within the paternal [sic] household. At the time, she had two family law proceedings she was navigating. And so I was quite worried about how she was coping. And so in the first family report, I had made recommendations around that to try and alleviate some of the stress, and there’s also potential carer burnout I was concerned about. And so it’s helpful for the mother to know that or to think about, if the children were to move towards having time with their father in a consistent rhythm, or routine, that is time where she may have time for herself with the other children there as well, but it may support her mental health, and that was my hope, to work towards reducing some of that level of stress that she’s experiencing, the carer burnout I was concerned about, and to overall provide her with an extra hand in parenting and support.
She also gave the following evidence:
[Ms P]: But also, I think because there’s such significant stresses in that maternal – in the maternal household, [X] and [Y] need a resilient or protective home where they – if there are quite a lot of risks in the maternal household – and it appeared to me, [X], in particular was being targeted by [K]. So they need an alternative home environment, aside from the maternal household, to have a sense of safety and security, as well as being in the maternal household to both help them develop resilience, and emotional wellbeing, but also to have extra eyes on them, extra safeguards, and for them to have a safe home, which, if there is chaos, or stress, or things happening in the maternal household, they have a safe and stable environment.
I accept all that evidence.

Relationships

The children have a right to know their father and to have a relationship with him in circumstances where I find that there are significant benefits to them of such a relationship, and that having such a relationship is safe for them.
The Panel Family Consultant gave evidence about the impacts on X and Y of having no time with their father:
[Ms P]: Well, the number one consequence is they won’t have a relationship with their father, so the impact of that is it can be future identity issues, it can be self-esteem issues, potential rejection and abandonment issues, confusion, and it can also increase risks around the mother’s relationship with the children. For example, [X] is obviously building a positive relationship with her father. So if the relationship was to sever, when they get – when children get to early adolescence, and they start becoming curious about who they are, and their family, and their identity, they can often vote with their own feet, and that can sometimes cause friction between the parent who hasn’t encouraged or promoted the relationship, and that can, you know, lead to rebellion, and that – for [X] and [Y], I think that concern is more so that the normal child I would assess, and that’s because of the environment that they’re in at the moment.
And further in relation to [X’s] and [Y’s] particular circumstances:
[Ms P]: And [X] will have memories of spending time with their father, so that door’s already been opened, where there will be questions. And if it’s the case where [Y] and [X] are in a situation, especially as teenagers, where they’re seeing their half-siblings spending time with their father, or their paternal family, there’s a real risk of anger, frustration, rebellion, for their relationship with their father not being promoted.
In relation to X, the Panel Family Consultant was asked by counsel for the ICL about the short-term impact of stopping time which has now been occurring for more than two years:
[Ms P]: It will have short-term impact. So the short-term impact is anxiety, emotional issues, and underlying behavioural outburst is anxiety, grief, and loss, but what will happen is, children of [X’s] age find it hard to verbally express their feelings and emotions, so they may act out, they may become aggressive, they may become withdrawn, and it will likely end with the mother having to manage those behavioural issues, emotional outbursts, and with everything else going on, there’s more concerns around that as well. So it’s really unlikely that [X] will verbalise her feelings at this age, but I think there will be quite a different – quite a number of different behaviours that will be seen, and that is particularly – like I’ve noted in her interview, she has – she raised concerns about how she feels in the maternal household. So for that relationship with the father to be severed, and for her not to have that safe foundation, I think the behavioural issues will be even greater.
I accept the evidence of the Panel Family Consultant set out above.
It is also important for the children to experience relationships with their paternal extended family, and I accept the Panel Family Consultant’s evidence that it is important that they have relationships with their three paternal siblings. X has spent time with her paternal siblings on a few occasions, but Y has only met them once, when he was very young. In the first Family Report, the Panel Family Consultant recorded that “[b]oth parents became emotional and cried when they spoke about [X’s] relationship with her paternal siblings, particularly [F]” and Ms Trevaskis said that she believed that X misses her paternal siblings. In oral submissions on behalf of Ms Trevaskis, it was put to me that Ms Trevaskis would facilitate a relationship with the children’s paternal siblings, even if orders were made for X and Y to spend no time with Mr Goodwin. The only evidence before me which would support such a submission was that Ms Trevaskis had, in January and April 2024, arranged or attempted to arrange for the children to meet through the paternal siblings’ mother. Ms Trevaskis did not give evidence that she intended to facilitate such time in the future. I reject the submission that I can be satisfied that, even if I made an order for no time with Mr Goodwin, the children would have relationships with their paternal siblings.

ORDERS

I am satisfied that it is in Y’s and X’s best interests for them to spend time with Mr Goodwin. As indicated above, I am satisfied that an arrangement where Y and X progress to unsupervised time with Mr Goodwin is one in which they are safe from harm. The risks to Ms Trevaskis’s emotional and psychological safety arising from her anxiety in relation to Mr Goodwin can be mitigated by the orders which I propose to make including in relation to changeover and communication. There are significant benefits to Y and X from spending regular time with their father, including the psychological benefits of having a relationship with him during their childhood, the “respite” benefits about which the Panel Family Consultant opined, and the benefits of having relationships with their paternal siblings and extended paternal family. Mr Goodwin has sound capacity to meet their needs.
I am satisfied that the spend time arrangements should, subject to a less prescriptive provision for the time that Y spends with Mr Goodwin in Stage One, be in accordance with the proposal of the ICL, and as adopted by Mr Goodwin. It is a carefully crafted proposal, which reflects the recommendations of the Panel Family Consultant, provides for the gradual, supervised introduction of Y to Mr Goodwin, and moves towards an alternate weekend and a week about school holidays arrangement. The Panel Family Consultant described a process whereby Y is introduced to Mr Goodwin and commences time with Mr Goodwin in a supervised context and in a way which is responsive to Y’s needs. She had recommended that Y’s time be for just half an hour, but she qualified that:
[Ms P]: I made that recommendation, again to go to my point about the six months being slow, no pressure, not stressful, aligned with [Y’s] comfortability. If he’s showing signs that he’s comfortable, it doesn’t need to be the half-hour, but I was just giving the parents the idea, that the time needs to be aligned with how he’s presenting, and how he’s developmentally feeling. And if it needs to be cut down the two hours into intervals to settle him in, then that will be recommended but if he’s not showing signs that he doesn’t [sic] need that, then yes.
I am satisfied having heard Mr Goodwin’s evidence that Mr Goodwin will prioritise Y’s needs in terms of how supervised time progresses, and will take the advice of the supervision service. I am also satisfied that Ms Trevaskis will act in accordance with the reasonable recommendations of the supervision service. Given that, at this stage, no one knows how Y will adjust, it would not be appropriate to prescribe the amount of time (within the two hours that X will be spending with Mr Goodwin) that Y should spend with his father. Until now, X’s time with Mr Goodwin has been once a fortnight. It is preferable if the first stage of time, particularly for Y, is more frequent, but it may be that the supervision centre is not able to accommodate weekly time. I have made orders for weekly time in Stage One in the hope that that frequency can be accommodated by the supervision centre.
I am satisfied, based on the evidence of the Panel Family Consultant, that a period of six months will be sufficient for Y and X to be ready for unsupervised time, and that they should then spend time with Mr Goodwin on the same basis. Such time should build up gradually in three-month intervals. Mr Goodwin agreed with the recommendation of the Panel Family Consultant that, when time first starts, the focus should be on his time with the children, and expressed his view that his other children should not be introduced until after a period of three months of unsupervised time. He sought that order and I am satisfied that it is in the children’s best interests. From there, the time will progress in three-monthly intervals so that overnight time commences when Y is four. School holiday time will commence when Y is nearly five years old. I am satisfied, based on the evidence of the Panel Family Consultant, that these arrangements are in the children’s best interests, being consistent with their developmental needs.
I was not asked to, and do not, make orders for regular telephone or similar contact, but have made an order which provides for videoconferencing between the children and the parent with whom they are not spending time on that parent’s birthday or on the children’s birthdays. In relation to regular telephone communication, the Panel Family Consultant’s evidence was clear, and I accept it:
[Ms P]: I think [Y’s] too young. In my experience, telephone contact can often become cumbersome and challenging for younger children in particular. I think when children are older, and they’re used to talking on the phone and that they enjoy that, then that works. But for younger children, I often find that the parent who’s in the home, tends to have to manage behaviours around phone calls and stress and anxiety. So I’m always a bit wary about phone calls, for younger children.
It is to Mr Goodwin’s credit that he accepts that the children should continue to live with Ms Trevaskis and that she should have sole parental responsibility and responsibility for long-term decision-making for the children. Given Ms Trevaskis’s anxiety and the complete absence of any co-parenting relationship, I am satisfied that is in the children’s best interests. I am satisfied that it is in the children’s best interests for Mr Goodwin to be informed in advance of major long-term decisions which Ms Trevaskis is planning to make for the children and given the opportunity to provide his input. Having to communicate with Mr Goodwin in this way will cause Ms Trevaskis some anxiety, but that will be mitigated by an order that she can nominate the means by which written communication between the parents will occur, and an order which limits written communication to issues in relation to the care, welfare and development of the children. Having seen Mr Goodwin in the witness box I am confident that he will communicate appropriately with Ms Trevaskis.
Both parents and the ICL consented to a change of name order such that X and Y will have the same surname, and one which includes both of their parents’ surnames. The form of the order that I make is slightly different from what was proposed but embraces that consensus – which is to the parents’ credit.
I am satisfied that the restraints which I will make are appropriate for the welfare of the children and, as set out above, I am satisfied that the restraints mitigate the risks to the children which might otherwise arise in Ms Trevaskis’s household. Other mitigating factors are Ms Trevaskis’s undoubted parenting capacity, her commitment to the children and her role as a parent, her good insight and her proven willingness to engage with appropriate professionals to overcome the significant difficulties which she has faced, and – to some extent at least – continues to face. That the children will now also have their father significantly in their lives is a further mitigating factor and otherwise in their best interests.
